State of California

Memorandum                                                A-79-1321

To    :  Sarah, Ted, Tom S.                    Date  :  Oct. 10, 1979


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Dwight

Subject:  Letter from David Nawi, Air Resources Board (ARB)


         All of you should have in your possession a copy of
Mr. Nawi's memorandum dated October 3, 1979, requesting
advice regarding Mr. James G. Leather's participation in
proceedings to amend ARB's agricultural burning regulations.
Set forth below is additional information obtained from
Mr. Nawi and Mr. Leathers that was not provided in the original package of materials.

     1.    Economic impact of the proposed regulations on
           Mr. Leathers' farming interests.

     Mr. Nawi indicated that the staff report on the proposed
regulations states that farmers, who conduct burnings on
their acreage, may have additional costs of $.30 per acre.
Mr. Leathers uses 220 of his 680 acres for rice production.
The costs to Mr. Leathers of burning the 220 acres would be
approximately $66.00 if the regulations were implemented.
If all 680 acres were subject to burning, the additional
costs to Mr. Leathers would be approximately $204.00.

     An additional cost to farmers imposed by the proposed
regulations is the $5.00 increase in permit fees to burn
acreage on "no-burn days." The proposed regulation would
also make it more difficult to receive these permits.
Mr. Leathers does not believe he will be affected because he
has never sought a permit to burn on a "non-burn" day and
does not expect to do so in the future.

     2.    Possible nexus between the Rice Research Board and
           the proposed regulations on agricultural burning.

     The Rice Research Board was established pursuant to a
state marketing order for the purposes of promoting the rice
industry.  Mr. Leathers is Chairman of the Board.  According
to Mr. Leathers and Mr. Nawi, the Rice Research Board did

Sarah, Ted, Tom S.
October 10, 1979
Page Two


not participate in the drafing of these regulations although
opportunities were presented by ARB to all interested parties,
including the Board, to do so.

     3.  Effect of the proposed regulations on Mr. Leathers'
          rice co-op.

     Mr. Leathers' rice crop is marketed by a rice co-op.
The proposed regulations will not affect the rice co-op
because it is not anticipated that farmer rice production
will be affected if the proposed regulations are implemented.

                              Analysis

     Mr. Leathers is not disqualified because his financial
interests will not be materially affected by the proposed
regulations. The maximum costs of the proposed regulation
to Mr. Leathers' farming business is approximately $204.
This effect is well below the standards used by the Commission
for determining materiality (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(1)).

     There is no nexus between Mr. Leathers' role as Chairman
of the Rice Research Board and the decisions regarding rice
burnings before the ARB. The Board did not assist ARB in
developing the proposed regulations. Although the rice
industry law later has taken position before the legislature
regarding rice burnings, Mr. Leathers is not aware of any
official positions that might have been taken by the Board
during this period.

     Even if a nexus was present or the proposed regulations
affected the Rice Research Board, Mr. Leathers is not dis-
qualified because the Board is a public agency. The Board
was established pursuant to a state marketing order and
board members are designated in the Department of Food &
Agriculture's Conflict of Interest Code.

     The decision will not affect the rice co-op that mar-
kets Mr. Leathers' rice crop. The co-op will market whatever
rice is provided to it by its members. Mr. Leathers and
Mr. Nawi do not force any reduction in rice productivity by
farmers as a result of these regulations. If rice production
is not affected by the regulations, it is not foreseeable
that the co-op will be affected.

Sarah, Ted, Tom S.
October 10, 1979
Page Three


Mr. Nawi needs a response by close of business today or
tomorrow morning at the latest. Interested persons should
get together with me this afternoon to discuss my conclusions
and proposed advice that Mr. Leathers may participate in the
proceedings amending ARB's agricultural burning regulations.

V. ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT OF PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO AGRICULTURAL BURNING
GUIDELINES AND METEOROLOGICAL CRITERIA FOR REGULATING AGRICULTURAL
BURNING

A. EMISSION IMPACT

The impact that the amendments proposed herein will have on

the environment in terms of emissions are of two kinds. Some of

the amendments are predicted to effect emission reductions; others

are predicted to cause a similar amount of pollutants to be emitted

into the atmosphere as is presently the case, but to do so under

more favorable atmospheric conditions. Still other amendments are

not expected to affect the quantity of pollutants being emitted,

but are being proposed to assist in the resolution of administrative

problems that have been identified.

Emission reductions are expected to be realized from three

components of these proposed amendments; those that (1) extend the

backfiring requirement, (2) specify a later hour for the burning

of certain field crop wastes, and (3) extend the application of

the rice burning regulations to the San Joaquin Valley.

The extension of the backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting

requirement to barley, oats and wheat in the Sacramento Valley Air

Basin and to these crops as well as to rice in the San Joaquin

Valley Air Basin is expected to reduce particulate matter emissions

by an estimated 2,051 tons per year in the Sacramento Valley Air

Basin and by an estimated 1,671 tons per year in the San Joaquin

Valley Air Basin. There is not expected to be a significant

reduction in the amounts of other pollutants emitted as a result

of this proposal.

The requirement that the burning of waste from field crops

not commence until 10:00 a.m. is expected to significantly reduce

State of California

Memorandum

To : David Nawi                                          Date : NOV. 15, 1979
     Air Resources Board

                                                         A-79-132

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dwight Dickerson

Subject: Air Resources Board Member, James G. Leathers - Possible Conflict
         of Interest

     The purpose of this memorandum is to confirm in writing my
telephone response of October 11, 1978, to your memorandum dated
September 20, 1979, seeking advice regarding the Act's disqualifica-
tion provision. The facts as I understand them were as follows.

     The Air Resources Board is considering amending its statewide
agricultural burning regulations. These amendments will affect
the procedures used by farmers who find it necessary to burn off
their fields and will limit the time available to them for burning.
The Air Resources Board staff report indicates that the economic
impact of these proposed regulations on growers would be between
$.15 and $.75 an acre. The estimated total economic impact on the
farming community is expected to be approximately $83,400. The
amendments contemplated by the Air Resources Board are presently
in effect for most rice growers in the Sacramento Valley at the
local level because of local air pollution control laws.

     Mr. James A. Leathers is a member of the Air Resources Board.
Mr. Leathers owns a 680 acre farm in the Sacramento Valley on
which he grows rice and wheat. Two hundred twenty of the 680
acres farmed by Mr. Leathers would be affected by the proposed Air
Resources Board burning regulations. Mr. Leathers is also a member
of the rice co-op and the California Rice Research Board, which
was established pursuant to a state marketing order for the purpose
of improving the production of rice. The question about which you
have asked our advice is whether Mr. Leathers is disqualified from
participating in the hearings because of his farming and related
interests.

     Government Code Section 87100 provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local govern-
ment shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt
to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.

David Nawi
Nov. 15, 1979
Page Two


Government Code Section 87103 provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000);

     (b)  Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000);

     (c)  Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by
or promised to the public official within twelve months prior
to the time when the decision is made; or

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds any
position of management.

     ...

     Mr. Leathers is therefore required to refrain from participation
in those decisions that may materially affect his farming business,
in which he has an investment and which is a source of income to
him provided by regulation that:

     (a)  The financial effect of a governmental decision on
a financial interest of a public official is material if the
decision will have a significant effect on the business entity,
real property or source of income in question.

     (b)  In determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the effects of a governmental decision will be significant
within the meaning of the general standard set forth in paragraph
(a), consideration should be given to the following factors:

          (1)  Whether, in the case of a business entity in
which the public official holds a direct or indirect
investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more or
in the case of a business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, employee,
trustee or holds any position of management, the effect
of the decision will be to increase or decrease:

David Nawi
Nov. 15, 1979
Page Three


                (A)  The annualized gross revenues by the
lesser of:

                1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);
or

                2.  One percent if it is one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more; or

                (B)  Annual net income by the lessee of:

                1.  Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); or

                2.  One half of one percent if it is one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more; or

                (C)  Current assets or liabilities by the
lesser of:

                1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);
or

                2.  One half of one percent if it is one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                Current assets are deemed to be decreased
by the amount of any expenses incurred as a
result of a governmental decision.

                            ...

     (3)  Whether, in the case of a source of income as
defined in Government Code Section 87103(c), of two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more received by or
promised to a public official within 12 months prior to
the time the decision is made:

                (A)  The effect of the decision will be to
directly increase or decrease the amount of income
(other than rents) to be received by the official
by one hundred dollars ($100) or more; or

                (B)  There is a nexus between the governmental
decision and the purpose for which the official
receives income; or

                (C)  In the case of a source of income which
is a business entity, the business entity will be
affected in a manner described in subsection (b)(1)
above; or

David Nawi
Nov. 15, 1979
Page Four


(D) If the source of income is not a business
entity, the decision will have a significant effect
on the source.

...

2 Cal. Adm. Code §19702

Mr. Leathers burns 220 acres. The economic impact of the
regulations on Mr. Leathers' farming business would be $165.00 per
year if he were to continue burning 220 acres (720 acres at $.75).
If he were to burn all his acreage (680 acres), the total economic
impact of the regulation on his farm would be $510.00 per year.
Absent some unusual circumstances which would show such effects to
be significant, they are not large enough to be "material" within
the meaning of the Act.

Mr. Leathers is Chairman of the Rice Research Board and has
received salary and reimbursement for expenses from the Board in
excess of $250. However, Section 82030(b)(2) excludes from the
definition of income:

Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received
from a state, local, or federal government agency and reimbur[ment]
for travel expenses and per diem received from bona fide
educational, academic or charitable organization;

The California Rice Research Board is a state agency. It was
created pursuant to a state marketing order, and its members are
presently designated in the Department of Food and Agriculture
Conflict of Interest Code. Salary and reimbursements received by
Mr. Leathers from the Board are therefore not income within the
meaning of the Act, and Mr. Leathers need not refrain from participat[ion]
in Air Resources Board decisions affecting the Rice Research Board.

The Farmer's Rice co-op is also a source of income to Mr. Lea[th]
but it is my understanding that your office will make a determinat[ion]
based on the additional information that may be provided to you,
as to whether the proposed regulations will have a material effect
on the co-op.

I hope that our advice regarding this matter was helpful. If
you have any questions regarding this matter, please do not hesitat[e]
to contact me.

Dear Mr. Austin:

This is in response to your letter concerning EPA's proposed action to restrict the issuance of special burn permits which allow agricultural burning on "no burn" days.

As you are aware, in the October 27, 1978 Federal Register, EPA proposed to disapprove parts of the agricultural and open burning regulations of many air pollution control districts which were submitted by the Air Resources Board (ARB) as revisions to California's State Implementation Plan (SIP). The October 27, 1978 proposal also included regulations previously approved as a part of the applicable SIP.

EPA's proposed disapproval action was taken in order to correct the SIP, which does not currently include assurances that the public health will be protected when special agricultural burning permits are issued. This proposed action would not restrict burning on designated burn days. However, restrictions would apply to the issuance of special burn permits by air pollution control officers, of special burn permits which are based solely on economic loss and absent of public health consideration. This lack of public health consideration conflicts with the Clean Air Act which requires the primary consideration of the SIP to be that of public health impacts, i.e., national ambient air quality standards.

Memorandum

To    : Dwight                                    Date  : October 3, 1979


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Ted

Subject: Letter from David Nawi, ARB


     Please call Mr. Nawi and talk about:

     1) The effect of the regs on Mr. Leathers' 680 acres -- cost,
inconvenience, effect on productivity, etc.

     2) Is Rice Research a source of income which will be materially
affected? Is there a nexus between the ARB decision and his activities
for Rice Research? I assume the decision will affect the amount of
income which Leathers will receive from Rice Research.

     3) Any effect on the co-op?

     I think there's a strong possibility we would disqualify him
under general standard but we should get answer first and have
a discussion with Sobel and Sarah on October 10.

State of California

Memorandum

To : Alice Hughes
    Fair Political Practices Commission
    1100 K Street
    Sacramento, CA 95804

Date : September 20, 1979

Subject : ARB Member, James G. Leathers
          Possible Conflict of
          Interest

From : Air Resources Board

     We request your opinion regarding a possible conflict
of interest of a newly appointed member to the Air Resources Board, James
Leathers. Mr. Leathers was appointed to the Board September 10, 1979,
and has just completed his statement of economic interest, a copy of
which I enclose.

     On October 12, 1979, the Board will consider amendments to its
agricultural burning regulations. These amendments, if adopted, will
likely have a significant impact on rice growing operations within the
state. The proposed amendments are set out and explained in the staff
report, a copy of which is enclosed for your reference.

     In light of the fact that Mr. Leathers is a farmer and has
substantial acreage devoted to rice growing, the question has arisen as
to whether or not he may participate in the Board's action on the proposed
regulatory changes. In addition to Mr. Leathers' activities as a rice
grower, he is the Chairman of the California Rice Research Board, con-
stituted pursuant to a state marketing order for the purpose of investi-
gating and improving the production of rice.

     I request that on the basis of this information and any other
information you may require, you provide us with a determination as to
whether or not Mr. Leathers must be disqualified from participation in
the Board's consideration of the proposed regulatory amendments. We
would like this determination as soon as possible and in any event prior
to October 12, 1979, if you can complete it in this time frame. (We
also intend to look into this question in the event you unable to
respond by October 12.)

     Please feel free to call on us for any further information you
may require. Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

                                        David Nawi
                                        David Nawi
                                        General Counsel

sections.question: Whether Mr. Leathers is disqualified from participating in the hearings because of his farming and related interests in connection with proposed Air Resources Board amendments to agricultural burning regulations.
sections.conclusion:
1) 
Significant reductions in particulate matter emissions 
can be effected in the Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley 
Air Basins by the employment of backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting 
techniques for the burning of wastes from certain cereal crops. 
The crops which most appropriately lend themselves to these techniques 
are barley, oats. rice and wheat. 
The local air pollution control districts (APCDs) need the 
flexibility to allow exemptions from the requirement to use one of 
these techniques in certain circumstances. 
These circumstances 
would include appropriate consideration for fire hazard. visibility 
obstruction. or cases of insufficient fuel to sustain a backfire. 
The districts should also be provided the flexibility to extend 
the applicability of this requirement to the waste from other 
crops which may demonstrate an emissions reduction if burned by 
backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting. 
2) 
A significant reduction in emissions from agricultural 
burning can be achieved if the burning of field crop wastes in the 
Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley Air Basins does not begin 
until 10:00 a.m. 
3) 
The Agricultural Burning Guidelines do not contain any 
guidance for the issuance of no-burn day permits other than the 
consideration of "imminent and substantial economic loss." The 
Guidelines should be modified to better protect the public health 
by adding ambient air quality criteria which limits burning on 
"no-burn" days to those days when the health-based air quality 
standards would not otherwise be exceeded. 
Information on exceedances 
of air quality standards on no-burn days in the districts in the 


burning, the staff believed that the mandates of the Guidelines 
should be reviewed, primarily with their impact on agricultural 
waste burning in the central valleys of California, in order to 
determine where they could be strengthened so that air quality 
there could be improved. 
This review was completed by rJovember 
1978 and culminated in the staff preparing a proposal for revising 
the existing Guidelines. 
In addition, the staff scheduled and 
held a series of workshops for the agricultural community at which 
the proposal was presented. 
The staff also appeared and discussed 
the proposal at the request of several agriculturally oriented 
groups.

sections.analysis:
Mr. Leathers is not disqualified because his financial 
interests will not be materially affected by the proposed 
regulations. 
The maximum costs of the proposed regulation 
to Mr. Leathers' farming business is approximately $204. 
This effect is well below the standards used by the Commission 
for determining materiality (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b)(1) ). 
There is no nexus between Mr. Leathers' role as Chairman 
of the Rice Research Board and the decisions regarding rice 
burnings before the ARB. 
The Board did not assist ARB in 
developing the proposed regulations. 
Although the rice 
industry law later has taken position before the legislature 
regarding rice burnings, Mr. Leathers is not aware of any 
official positions that might have been taken by the Board 
during this period. 
Even if a nexus was present or the proposed regulations 
affected the Rice Research Board, Mr. Leathers is not dis-
qualified because the Board is a public agency. 
The Board 
was established pursuant to a state marketing order and 
board members are designated in the Department of Food & 
Agriculture's Conflict of Interest Code. 
The decision will not affect the rice co-op that mar-
kets Mr. Leathers' rice crop. 
The co-op will market whatever 
rice is provided to it by its memberr. 
Mr. Leathers and 
Mr. Nawi do not force any reduction in rice productivity by 
farmers as a result of these regulations. 
If rice production 
is not affected by the regulations, it is not foreseeable 
that the co-op will be affected.

We request your oplnlon regarding a possible,conflict of 
interest of a newly appointed member to the Air Resources Board, James 
Leathers. Mr. Leathers was appointed to the Board September 10, 1979, 
and has just completed his statement of economic interest, a copy of 
which I enclose. 
On October 12, 1979, the Board will consider amendments to its 
agricultural burning regulations. 
These amendments, if adopted, will 
likely have a significant impact on rice growing operations within the 
state. The proposed amendments are set out and explained in the staff 
report, a copy of which is enclosed for your reference. 
In light of the fact that Mr. Leathers is a farmer and has 
substantial acreage devoted to rice growing, the question has arisen as 
to whether or not he may participate in the Board's action on the proposed 
regulatory changes. 
In addition to Mr. Leathers' activities as a rice 
grower, he is the Chairman of the California Rice Research Board, con-
stituted pursuant to a state marketing ord~r for the purpose of investi-
gating and improving the production of rice. 
I request that on the basis of this information and any other 
information you may require, you provide us with a determination as to 
whether or not Mr. Leathers must be disqualified from participation in 
the Board's consideration of the proposed regulatory amendments. 
We 
would like this determination as soon as possible and in any event prior 
to October 12, 1979, if you can complete it in this time frame. 
(We 
also intend to look into this question in the event you are unable to 
respond by October 12.) 
Please feel free to call on us for any further information you 
may require. 
Thank you for your assistance in this matter· 
D~~' 
David Nawi
sections.question_synthetic: Is James G. Leathers, an Air Resources Board member who owns farmland and has interests in rice-related organizations, disqualified from participating in ARB proceedings to amend agricultural burning regulations that would affect his farming operations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Leathers is not disqualified from participating in the ARB proceedings because the proposed regulations would not have a materially significant financial effect on his farming business (estimated impact of only $204) and there is no connection between his Rice Research Board chairmanship and the ARB decisions.

Key sections clearly identify the question and conclusion regarding materiality of financial interest under Government Code Sections 87100-87103.

QUESTION: Whether Mr. Leathers is disqualified from participating in the hearings because of his farming and related interests in connection with proposed Air Resources Board amendments to agricultural burning regulations.

CONCLUSION: 1) 
Significant reductions in particulate matter emissions 
can be effected in the Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley 
Air Basins by the employment of backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting 
techniques for the burning of wastes from certain cereal crops. 
The crops which most appropriately lend themselves to these techniques 
are barley, oats. rice and wheat. 
The local air pollution control districts (APCDs) need the 
flexibility to allow exemptions from the requirement to use one of 
these techniques in certain circumstances. 
These circumstances 
would include appropriate consideration for fire hazard. visibility 
obstruction. or cases of insufficient fuel to sustain a backfire. 
The districts should also be provided the flexibility to extend 
the applicability of this requirement to the waste from other 
crops which may demonstrate an emissions reduction if burned by 
backfiring or into-the-wind striplighting. 
2) 
A significant reduction in emissions from agricultural 
burning can be achieved if the burning of field crop wastes in the 
Sacramento Valley and San Joaquin Valley Air Basins does not begin 
until 10:00 a.m. 
3) 
The Agricultural Burning Guidelines do not contain any 
guidance for the issuance of no-burn day permits other than the 
consideration of "imminent and substantial economic loss." The 
Guidelines should be modified to better protect the public health 
by adding ambient air quality criteria which limits burning on 
"no-burn" days to those days when the health-based air quality 
standards would not otherwise be exceeded. 
Information on exceedances 
of air quality standards on no-burn days in the districts in the 


burning, the staff believed that the mandates of the Guidelines 
should be reviewed, primarily with their impact on agricultural 
waste burning in the central valleys of California, in order to 
determine where they could be strengthened so that air quality 
there could be improved. 
This review was completed by rJovember 
1978 and culminated in the staff preparing a proposal for revising 
the existing Guidelines. 
In addition, the staff scheduled and 
held a series of workshops for the agricultural community at which 
the proposal was presented. 
The staff also appeared and discussed 
the proposal at the request of several agriculturally oriented 
groups.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum A-79-1321 To : Sarah, Ted, Tom S. Date : Oct. 10, 1979 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Dwight Subject: Letter from David Nawi, Air Resources Board (ARB) All of you should have in your possession a copy of Mr. Nawi's memorandum dated October 3, 1979, requesting advice regarding Mr. James G. Leather's participation in proceedings to amend ARB's agricultural burning regulations. Set forth below is additional information obtained from Mr. Nawi and Mr. Leathers that was not provided in the original package of materials. 1. Economic impact of the proposed regulations on Mr. Leathers' farming interests. Mr. Nawi indicated that the staff report on the proposed regulations states that farmers, who conduct burnings on their acreage, may have additional costs of $.30 per acre. Mr. Leathers uses 220 of his 680 acres for rice production. The costs to Mr. Leathers of burning the 220 acres would be approximately $66.00 if the regulations were implemented. If all 680 acres were subject to burning, the additional costs to Mr. Leathers would be approximately $204.00. An additional cost to farmers imposed by the proposed regulations is the $5.00 increase in permit fees to burn acreage on "no-burn days." The proposed regulation would also make it more difficult to receive these permits. Mr. Leathers does not believe he will be affected because he has never sought a permit to burn on a "non-burn" day and does not expect to do so in the future. 2. Possible nexus between the Rice Research Board and the proposed regulations on agricultural burning. The Rice Research Board was established pursuant to a state marketing order for the purposes of promoting the rice industry. Mr. Leathers is Chairman of the Board. According to Mr. Leathers and Mr. Nawi, the Rice Research Board did Sarah, Ted, Tom S. October 10, 1979 Page Two not participate in the drafing of these regulations although opportunities were presented by ARB to all interested parties, including the Board, to do so. 3. Effect of the proposed regulations on Mr. Leathers' rice co-op. Mr. Leathers' rice crop is marketed by a rice co-op. The proposed regulations will not affect the rice co-op because it is not anticipated that farmer rice production will be affected if the proposed regulations are implemented. Analysis Mr. Leathers is not disqualified because his financial interests will not be materially affected by the proposed regulations. The maximum costs of the proposed regulation to Mr. Leathers' farming business is approximately $204. This effect is well below the standards used by the Commission for determining materiality (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)). There is no nexus between Mr. Leathers' role as Chairman of the Rice Research Board and the decisions regarding rice burnings before the ARB. The Board did not assist ARB in developing the proposed regulations. Although the rice industry law later has taken position before the legislature regarding rice burnings, Mr. Leathers is not aware of any official positions that might have been taken by
embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to an inquiry about whether Air Resources Board member James G. Leathers has a disqualifying conflict of interest in participating in proceedings to amend agricultural burning regulations. The FPPC concludes he is not disqualified because the financial impact on his farming operations is immaterial and there is no meaningful nexus to his other interests.
